title: Multi-file builds

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Multi-file builds; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A multi-file C build compiles each translation unit with compatible declarations and then links the selected objects and libraries under reproducible flags.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Changing a public header recompiles every dependent source file before linking, preventing old object code from using an incompatible declaration. Your goal is to build and diagnose a multi-file command-line program using a repeatable build description. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Failure-safe cleanup; Scope, linkage and storage duration; Header interfaces and include guards; Preprocess, compile, assemble and link. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A multi-file C build compiles each translation unit with compatible declarations and then links the selected objects and libraries under reproducible flags. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Header dependency tracking, ABI flags, library order, duplicate definitions, stale objects, and conditional macros can make incremental builds inconsistent. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Only the .c file directly edited needs recompilation, even when a shared header changed its declarations.

includes: Compile separate translation units and link reproducibly with declared dependencies.
Build and diagnose a multi-file command-line program using a repeatable build description.
excludes: Header dependency tracking, ABI flags, library order, duplicate definitions, stale objects, and conditional macros can make incremental builds inconsistent.

outcomes: Build and diagnose a multi-file command-line program using a repeatable build description.

examples.title: Multi-file builds: defined case
examples.situation: Changing a public header recompiles every dependent source file before linking, preventing old object code from using an incompatible declaration.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Changing a public header recompiles every dependent source file before linking, preventing old object code from using an incompatible declaration.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Track header dependencies and rebuild every affected translation unit before linking a coherent program.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Track header dependencies and rebuild every affected translation unit before linking a coherent program.

examples.title: Multi-file builds: boundary case
examples.situation: A clean strict build uses the same language standard and warning flags in local and CI environments.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A clean strict build uses the same language standard and warning flags in local and CI environments.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Header dependency tracking, ABI flags, library order, duplicate definitions, stale objects, and conditional macros can make incremental builds inconsistent.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Only the .c file directly edited needs recompilation, even when a shared header changed its declarations.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Build and diagnose a multi-file command-line program using a repeatable build description.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Only the .c file directly edited needs recompilation, even when a shared header changed its declarations.
misconceptions.correction: Track header dependencies and rebuild every affected translation unit before linking a coherent program.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Only the .c file directly edited needs recompilation, even when a shared header changed its declarations.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Multi-file builds: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Header dependency tracking, ABI flags, library order, duplicate definitions, stale objects, and conditional macros can make incremental builds inconsistent.
